control_checks.control_reason: MCP tools should declare standard annotations so clients can present better approval UI and feed policy engines with consistent risk hints.
Annotations from untrusted servers must not reduce friction for sensitive, state-changing, or external tools.
Tool descriptions and schemas can become an agent supply-chain path; drift after approval must force re-review.
Structured output validation limits accidental or malicious content from reaching the model without inspection.
Open-world tools can return attacker-controlled content and should taint the session before later tools run.
Sessions that combine private data, untrusted content, and external communication create a realistic data-theft path.
Destructive or non-idempotent tools need explicit user or reviewer confirmation with a durable approval record.
Runtime tool calls must remain inside the workflow's declared namespace and access mode.
Tool list changes after approval can invalidate a previous trust decision and should force a re-review or session reset.

enterprise_adoption_packet.board_level_claim: SecurityRecipes can prevent tool-combination risk from becoming unmanaged agentic exfiltration risk.
default_questions_answered: Which MCP namespaces expose private context, untrusted content, state changes, or external communication?
Which tools can rely on annotations because the server is trusted, and which annotations are only UI hints?
Which workflows can form a private-data plus untrusted-content plus external-communication path?
What runtime fields must appear before a tool-risk decision is trusted?
Where should premium hosted controls live: live tool-list monitoring, annotation drift detection, tool schema signing, and fleet-wide session-combination blocking?
enterprise_adoption_packet.recommended_first_use: Run this pack during MCP server intake, agent-host design, gateway policy review, tool approval UI design, and enterprise buyer diligence.
enterprise_adoption_packet.sales_motion: Lead with the open risk contract, then sell hosted tool-risk monitoring, signed annotation inventory, tool-list drift alerts, and session-level exfiltration-path blocking for production MCP fleets.

session_combination_rule.description: A session must not simultaneously have private-data access, untrusted-content exposure, and external communication without an explicit approved control path.

intent: Define a SecurityRecipes MCP tool-risk contract that turns MCP tool annotations, connector trust, authorization conformance, workflow scope, and runtime session context into deterministic allow, hold, deny, or kill decisions before an agent invokes a tool.

positioning.buyer: AI Platform, Product Security, MCP platform, AppSec, IAM, observability, procurement security, and enterprise architecture teams
positioning.category: MCP tool-risk governance and session-level exfiltration prevention
positioning.enterprise_promise: SecurityRecipes can explain whether an MCP tool call is safe enough for a governed agent session using trusted tool metadata, connector evidence, workflow scope, and combination-risk checks instead of relying on model judgment.
positioning.why_now: MCP servers now expose tool annotations and richer tool schemas, but the protocol is explicit that annotations are untrusted hints unless the server is trusted. Enterprises need a policy layer that consumes those hints without confusing them for enforcement, especially when sessions combine private data access, untrusted content, and external communication.

residual_risks.risk: Tool annotations are not enforcement and can be false on untrusted servers.
residual_risks.treatment: Use this contract as a policy input, then enforce hard guarantees through gateway scope, sandboxing, authorization, network controls, and output validation.

residual_risks.risk: The risk of a tool depends on the rest of the session, not only on that tool's declaration.
residual_risks.treatment: Evaluate private-data, untrusted-content, and external-communication factors before each tool call and after tool-list changes.

residual_risks.risk: MCP annotation proposals are still evolving.
residual_risks.treatment: Version the profile, keep standard annotations conservative, and use namespaced metadata only as deployment-specific evidence until ecosystem-wide annotations stabilize.

risk_tiers.description: Read-only public or open-reference context with no private data, state change, or external communication path.
risk_tiers.title: Context Only

risk_tiers.description: Read-only sources that may carry untrusted content and must taint the session before later tools are called.
risk_tiers.title: Read Untrusted Content

risk_tiers.description: Internal findings, repos, tickets, logs, SBOMs, or identity context that can become sensitive when combined with output or external tools.
risk_tiers.title: Private Context

risk_tiers.description: Scoped branch, ticket, or workflow state changes that are allowed only inside declared workflow boundaries and reviewer-gated outputs.
risk_tiers.title: State Changing

risk_tiers.description: Approval-required, destructive, external-communication, or irreversible operations that need explicit control evidence before use.
risk_tiers.title: Destructive or Exfiltration Capable

implementation: Runtime evaluator for annotation, scope, and session-combination decisions.
Human-readable docs page and MCP tool exposure.
selected_feature.reason: Enterprise MCP buyers need a way to use tool annotations as risk vocabulary without mistaking them for enforcement, while blocking session-level exfiltration paths before an agent acts.

standards_alignment.coverage: Tool discovery, invocation, input and output schemas, human confirmation expectations, tool result validation, logging, and the requirement that clients treat annotations as untrusted unless they come from trusted servers.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Tools specification

standards_alignment.coverage: Current annotation semantics, cautious defaults, annotation limitations, trust requirements, session-combination risk, and the emerging interest-group direction for richer tool risk metadata.
standards_alignment.name: MCP Tool Annotations as Risk Vocabulary

standards_alignment.coverage: OAuth 2.1 resource servers, protected-resource metadata, resource indicators, audience-bound tokens, token validation, HTTPS, PKCE, and token-passthrough denial.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Authorization specification

standards_alignment.coverage: Confused-deputy prevention, token passthrough, SSRF, session hijacking, local-server compromise, scope minimization, and auditability for MCP implementations.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Security Best Practices

standards_alignment.coverage: Form and URL elicitation modes, user approval semantics, sensitive-information restrictions, URL-mode security, and explicit accept, decline, and cancel states.
standards_alignment.name: Model Context Protocol Elicitation specification

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent behavior hijacking, tool misuse and exploitation, identity and privilege abuse, human trust manipulation, memory and context poisoning, and rogue-agent containment.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026

standards_alignment.coverage: Threat-model-based agentic AI risks, mitigations, autonomous tool use, context manipulation, multi-agent boundaries, and operational safeguards.
standards_alignment.name: OWASP Agentic AI Threats and Mitigations

standards_alignment.coverage: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage functions for AI lifecycle risk, including accountability, operational monitoring, and risk treatment.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework

standards_alignment.coverage: Generative AI lifecycle, data provenance, third-party dependency, measurement, monitoring, cybersecurity, incident, and operational risk management.
standards_alignment.name: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile

standards_alignment.coverage: Agent discovery, Agent Cards, authentication and authorization, secondary credentials, and secure context exchange between opaque agents.
standards_alignment.name: Agent2Agent Protocol specification
